cards.text: Exceptions provide a way to handle {{c1::errors}} that separate error handling from normal code flow, allowing functions to {{c2::return values}} while still signaling errors.

cards.front: What are the three main components of C++ exception handling?
cards.back: throw (raising exceptions), try (detecting exceptions), and catch (handling exceptions).

cards.text: Best practice: Throw exceptions by {{c1::value}}, catch them by {{c2::const reference}}, and derive custom exception classes from {{c3::std::exception}}.

cards.front: Why is RAII critical for exception safety?
cards.back: RAII ensures resources are automatically cleaned up during stack unwinding when exceptions are thrown, preventing resource leaks.

cards.text: Destructors should {{c1::never throw}} exceptions. If a destructor throws during stack unwinding, {{c2::std::terminate()}} is called.

cards.front: When should you use exceptions vs other error handling methods?
cards.back:
Use exceptions for:
  • Exceptional circumstances, not normal flow
  • Errors that need to propagate through call stack
  • Constructor failures
  • Deep call chains where return codes are unwieldy
Avoid for performance-critical code with frequent errors.

cards.text: Mark functions {{c1::noexcept}} when they won't throw, especially {{c2::move constructors}}, {{c3::move assignment}}, and {{c4::destructors}}. This enables STL optimizations.

cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines for robust exception handling:
  • Use RAII for all resource management
  • Throw by value, catch by const reference
  • Derive from std::exception
  • Don't throw from destructors
  • Mark move ops noexcept when possible
  • Use exceptions for exceptional cases only
  • Order catch blocks from specific to general
  • Wrap main() in try-catch

cards.front: What is stack unwinding and when does it occur?
cards.back: Stack unwinding is the process of calling destructors for all local objects and removing function calls from the call stack when searching for an exception handler.

cards.text: The {{c1::basic}} exception safety guarantee ensures no resource leaks and valid state. The {{c2::strong}} guarantee ensures either complete success or no effect. The {{c3::nothrow}} guarantee ensures no exceptions are thrown.

cards.front: Why do STL containers use std::move_if_noexcept during reallocation?
cards.back: To maintain the strong exception guarantee by using move when safe (noexcept) and copy otherwise, ensuring the original data remains intact if an exception is thrown.

cards.text: Exception handling separates {{c1::error handling}} from normal code, uses {{c2::stack unwinding}} to find handlers, requires {{c3::RAII}} for safety, and should be combined with {{c4::noexcept}} for optimization.
